texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: Carbon dioxide emissions to the atmosphere lead to global warming and unpredictable climate change via the greenhouse effect. Carbon dioxide from industrial sources can be captured by the membrane separation technique to mitigate the greenhouse effect. In this research, a polymer solution was prepared by blending a cellulose triacetate (CTA) polymer with a trin -butyl phosphate (TBP) additive. The polymer solution was coated on an alumina ( α -Al2O3) tube, which acted as a support material, to prepare a composite membrane for the CO2 separation. The composite membrane that was prepared was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA), and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analyses. The CO2 permeance and the selectivity for the prepared composite membrane were evaluated using a constant pressure-variable volume method. The influence of the concentration of CTA and TBP, polymer solution preparation time, number of sequential dip coating, and the feed gas pressure on the CO2, N2, and CH4 gas separation performances was examined. The highest CO2 permeance of 129 GPU and the selectivity of 19.9 versus N2 and 10.6 against CH4 were obtained for the pure gases, and a CO2 permeance of 116 GPU and selectivity of 17.1 against N2 were obtained for a mixture of gases (15% CO2/85% N2). texts.orig: A n increase in the emissions of greenhouse gases (GHGs), such as CO2, CH4, N2O, NO2, SO2, chlorofluorocarbons (CFC), etc., into the atmosphere is causing global environment problems such as global warming and irregular climate change. Carbon dioxide is the major contributor ( /223c 60%) to global warming among other GHGs. 1 Although the increase in the atmospheric CO2 concentration is caused by both anthropogenic and natural emissions, almost 80% of CO2 emissions are being caused by the former. The principal anthropogenic sources of CO2 are industrial flue (i.e. coal-fired power plant, steel production, and cement manufacturing industries etc.)

texts.orig: Absorption, adsorption, chemical looping, membrane separation, and cryogenic separation are some of the techniques available for CO2 separation. 3,4 Among them, membrane-based gas separation is an excellent method due to its low energy input, the ease with which it can be scaled up, and its lower environmental impact, which make it preferable for bulk gas separation. An increasing interest in membrane-based gas separation and purification is evident from a large number of publications on this subject in recent years. 5-7 Among the various type of membranes, composite membranes have been gaining increasing attention owing to their potential application for CO2 separation. A composite membrane with porous support and a thin selective layer on the top of the support has wide industrial applications due to its relatively higher gas permeation performance and mechanical strength. Many authors have focused on the preparation of composite membranes to study the effectiveness of the prepared composite membrane for CO2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ebadi Amooghin et al. 8 prepared composite membranes of MEA-PVA/GA/PTFE and DEA-PVA/GA/PTFE, and investigated the CO2/N2 separation performance of the prepared composite membranes for both a pure gas and a mixture of gases. They reported that the CO2/N2 separation performance of the DEA-PVA membranes was higher than that of MEA-PVA membranes, and the cross-linked membranes showed more CO2/N2 permselectivities when compared to uncross-linked membranes. They said that overall performance of composite membranes (DEA-PVA/GA/PTFE) was higher with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 122.04 (pure gas) and 907.06 (a mixture of gas). Similarly, Arijit Mondal and Bishnupada Mandal 9 prepared a novel CO2-selective thin-film composite membrane (cross-linked PVA/PVP/suitable amine/PS) and studied the transport properties of CO2 and N2 (N2 and CO2 flux, CO2/N2 selectivity) through the membrane. The authors obtained a maximum CO2 permeability of 1396 barrer and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 370 for the composite membrane with 40 µ mactive layer thickness at 2.8 atm feed-side absolute pressure and 100 ° C.

texts.orig: The reported composite membranes for the CO2 separation are mostly porous polymeric support. The main disadvantage of polymeric support is low thermal, mechanical, and chemical stability. 10 The application of ceramic supports could avoid the drawbacks related to the polymeric support by the enhancement of membrane stability via confinement effect, which can also provide adequate mechanical strength to the thin selective layer, even at a higher pressure and temperature. Ceramic supports have therefore gained scientific attention in the preparation of composite membranes. 11-14

texts.orig: Liu et al. 15 prepared ceramic-supported polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA) composite membranes. Then, the author evaluated the gas separation performance for the pure gases (CO2, N2, H2) and the binary gas mixtures (CO2/N2 and CO2/H2). They reported that the gas separation performance of the ceramic supported composite membranes for the CO2, N2, and H2 gases was better than the polymeric supported composite membranes.

It was inferred that both of the membranes showed good permeation ca. 20 000 GPU with a selectivity of ca. 2.

Meredith et al. 20 used Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy to study the CO2 interaction with three Lewis bases, i.e. triethylamine (TEA), pyridine (PYR), and TBP. They demonstrated a strong interaction between CO2 and TBP compared to triethylamine and pyridine. Hao et al. 21 studied the solubility of CO2 in TBP, methanol, and N -methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP). The author reported that CO2 is more soluble in TBP than in methanol and NMPsolvents. Moreover, the author used GC/MS and FTIR analyses to confirm CO2 interaction with TBP via physical absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this research, an attempt has been made to produce novel low-cost ceramic-supported CTA and TBP-CTA composite membranes. To the best of our knowledge, the use of CTA-TBP composite membranes for gas separation has not been discussed elsewhere in the literature. In this study, key variables - e.g. influence of CTA and TBP concentration, polymer solution preparation time, number of sequential dip coating and the trans-membrane pressure - have been scrutinized systematically to obtain the maximum CO2 permeance and selectivity against N2 and CH4 gases. Further, the gas separation performance of the composite membranes on the binary gas mixture (15% CO2 and 85% N2) was examined.

texts.orig: The melting temperature (Tm) and the glass transition temperature (Tg) for the prepared membranes were determined by using DSC analysis. The prepared membrane was also examined to measure Tg values using the TA DSC Q200 thermal analyzer (TA Instruments, Delaware, United States). The gas-permeation experiment on the mixture of gases was carried out in continuous flow mode with the help of a partially opened retentate valve. The feed gas stream consisting of a mixture of CO2 and N2 gases was passed through the canister before entering the gas permeation unit. The composition of the permeate gas exiting from the permeation unit was determined by using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Netel) equipped with the thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a Haysep 'D' packed column. Helium gas was used as a carrier gas and the injected gas sample volume was 1 mL. The GC was calibrated using the standard gas mixture once in a week to ensure the reliability of the gas permeation data. texts.orig: showed the bending of the C-CH3 group. The characteristic peaks around 1237 and 1052 cm -1 were attributed to the asymmetric stretching of ether C-O and C-O-C vibrations. 23 The incorporation of TBP in the CTA polymer matrix (Fig. 2c and d) resulted in the improved intensity of the pure CTA spectra. The peaks in the spectrum (c) of M2B appeared at 3473 cm -1 , 2963 cm -1 , and 2875 cm -1 were associated with the stretching vibration of the O-H, C-H in CH2, and -CH3 in TBP, respectively. Moreover, the spectral broadening was observed in the region of 1237 cm -1 and 1051 cm -1 for the M2B composite film. This was due to the overlapping of stretching vibrations of both CTA and TBP (P = O, C-O, and P-O-C, C-O-C) in the same region. 24 It was suggested that the interaction between the TBP and the CTA did not form any new covalent bonds, but only secondary interactions, e.g. hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions, were observed between the acetyl groups of CTA and the functional groups of the TBP during the film formation. 25 Similarly, in the FTIR spectrum of M2B composite membrane before and after the gas permeation experiment, no significant changes were observed in the absorption bands and the peak intensity. It was confirmed that the membrane was stable after the gas permeation evaluation without any notable damage.

texts.orig: The DSC curves of M20 and M2B membranes are shown in Fig. 5. It can be observed from Fig. 5a that there are two endothermic peaks at 76 ° C and 307 ° C. The one endothermic peak at 76 ° C was due to the evaporation of water / solvent and the other one at 307 ° C was due to the melting of the CTA polymer. The glass transition temperature (Tg) of the M20 membrane was observed at 236 ° C. In Fig. 5b (M2B membrane) one large endothermic peak at 141 ° C and another small endothermic peak at 254 ° C with one exothermic peak at 296 ° C were observed. The first peak might be due to the decomposition of TBP and the second peak was due to the melting of the CTA polymer and the exothermic peak might be attributed to the combustion of melted CTA, where Tg of the M2B membrane was obtained nearly at 217 ° C. 28 Figure 5 makes it clear that there was a small reduction in the Tg of pure CTA by the addition of TBP into the CTA polymer matrix. This was due to the addition of TBP , which disturbed the CTA polymer chain resulting in the mobility of polymer chains and subsequently reducing the Tg. 29
texts.text: The DSC curves of M20 and M2B membranes are shown in Fig. 5. It can be observed from Fig. 5a that there are two endothermic peaks at 76 ° C and 307 ° C. The one endothermic peak at 76 ° C was due to the evaporation of water / solvent and the other one at 307 ° C was due to the melting of the CTA polymer. The glass transition temperature (Tg) of the M20 membrane was observed at 236 ° C. In Fig. 5b (M2B membrane) one large endothermic peak at 141 ° C and another small endothermic peak at 254 ° C with one exothermic peak at 296 ° C were observed. The first peak might be due to the decomposition of TBP and the second peak was due to the melting of the CTA polymer and the exothermic peak might be attributed to the combustion of melted CTA, where Tg of the M2B membrane was obtained nearly at 217 ° C. 28 Figure 5 makes it clear that there was a small reduction in the Tg of pure CTA by the addition of TBP into the CTA polymer matrix. This was due to the addition of TBP , which disturbed the CTA polymer chain resulting in the mobility of polymer chains and subsequently reducing the Tg. 29

The results showed that the bare α -alumina ceramic tube without the CTA polymer coating had a high CO2 permeance of ca. 30 000 GPU, but it had a negligible CO2 selectivity of 0.95 and 0.89 against both N2 and CH4 gases, respectively. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SEM images revealed that when the solution preparation time was 3 h, the composite membrane that was formed was porous. On the other hand, when the coating solution preparation time was beyond 3 h, the prepared composite membrane became non-porous. This might be the reason for the increase in the intermolecular bonding between the CTA polymer and the additive TBP while increasing the solution preparation or stirring time, causing more membrane compactness, which in turn reduced both pore size and porosity of the membrane. Hence, an increase in the solution preparation or stirring time changed the membrane morphology completely, from porous to non-porous. As a result, gas diffusivity across the composite membrane was drastically reduced. For further studies, the coating solution preparation or stirring time was therefore fixed at 3 h.

texts.orig: Effect of the number of sequential dip coating. For our preliminary studies, the number of sequential dip coatings of the membrane was three. The number of dip coatings influence the membrane's gas separation performance. 22 To evaluate the effect of the number of CTA-TBP sequential coatings on the gas-separation performance of the M2B composite membrane, the polymer solution was coated on the ceramic support by single, double, triple, and multi-sequential dip coating and the gas separation performance of the composite membranes is illustrated in Fig. 12. texts.orig: Pure gas analysis. The effect of feed gas pressure on CO2 permeation of M2B with composite membranes that had been dip coated twice was investigated in the range of 2 to 7 bar of the feed gas. The results illustrated in Fig. 13a revealed that the flux of CO2 and other gases increased as the feed gas pressure increased, and the rate of increase of the flux for CO2 was much higher than for N2 and CH4. In other words, the CO2 selectivity of the composite membrane increased with an increase in feed gas pressure because of the higher flux of CO2 compared to other gases, i.e. N2 and CH4. Carbon dioxide follows an adsorptive-type mechanism in the presence of TBP at room temperature, which results in high CO2 flux compared to non-adsorbable gases - namely N2 and CH4. 21

In the gas mixture, CO2 permeance through the M2B composite membrane, dip coated twice, decreased with increasing partial pressure difference across the membrane, but the CO2 selectivity versus N2 increased. Similar observations were made in the pure gas permeation study. Maximum CO2 permeance of 116 GPU and the selectivity of 17.1 versus. N2 were observed at the CO2 partial pressure difference of 0.15 bar. When the CO2 partial pressure difference was increased to 0.9 bar, CO2 permeance was reduced to 90 GPU with a slight increase in the CO2 selectivity of 19.2 against N2. This result suggested that the CO2 separation performance from the mixture of gases was more significant at its higher partial pressure difference than the lower one. It could happen that at the higher CO2 partial pressure difference, the higher CO2 adsorption blocked the micro-pore diffusion pathway for N2 and the compression of the polymer chain in the composite membrane greatly reduced the permeance of N2 rather than CO2, increasing CO2 selectivity over N2. 41

texts.orig: The prepared M20 and M2B composite membranes were compared with the other reported cellulose acetate (CA)-based membranes in the literature for CO2 permeance and selectivity against N2 and CH4. The comparison data are given in Table 4. Compared to other CA-based membranes, the prepared M2B composite membrane had higher CO2 permeance and good selectivity for CO2. The good CO2 separation performance of the prepared composite membrane had some advantages over the other CA membranes. First, the thickness of the M2B composite membrane (9 µ m) was much lower than that of the other reported CA membranes. texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide permeance and selectivity against N2 and CH4 was demonstrated using a tubular α -Al2O3 ceramic-supported CTA-TBP membrane under various operating conditions. This composite membrane showed a higher CO2 selectivity of 19.9 versus N2, compared to the pure CTA membrane (0.96 versus N2). Under optimized reaction conditions - i.e. 3% w/v of CTA, 15% w/v of TBP (M2B), polymer solution preparation time of 3 h, membrane thickness of 9 µ m(two time dip-coating), feed gas pressure of 2 bar - a maximum CO2 permeance of 129 GPU and selectivity of 19.9 against N2 and 10.6 against CH4 were achieved for the pure gases. In the case of a gas mixture (15% CO2 and 85% N2), the highest CO2 permeance of 116 GPU and selectivity of 17.1 versus N2 were attained. The highest CO2 permeance and the acceptable selectivity were obtained at the lower CO2 partial pressure difference of 0.15 bar for the prepared composite membrane, which is the preferred condition for CO2 separation from industrial flue gases. In conclusion, the TBP-CTA/ α -Al2O3 composite membrane can be used for CO2 separation from CH4 (fuel gas) and N2 (flue gas). However, further study needs to be conducted to improve the CO2 selectivity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kunalan Shankar is a PhD student in the Centre for Environmental Studies at the Anna University in Chennai under the guidance of Dr K. Palanivelu. His research area is carbon dioxide separation from flue and fuel gases using composite membranes, which is a topic currently attracting the attention of much research. He pursued his BSc and MSc in chemistry at the University of Madras, India in 2008 and 2011.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Palanivelu Kandasamy is a professor in Centre for Environmental Studies at the Anna University in Chennai-25, and also the director of the Centre for Climate Change and Adaptation Research at the Anna University in Chennai-25. His research areas include recovery of valuable products from wastewater and recovery of heavy metals from e-waste using supported liquid membranes technique, separation, and utilization of carbon dioxide, climate change, and adaptation research. He received his PhD in environmental analytical chemistry from IIT Madras, India in 1991.
texts.text: Palanivelu Kandasamy is a professor in Centre for Environmental Studies at the Anna University in Chennai-25, and also the director of the Centre for Climate Change and Adaptation Research at the Anna University in Chennai-25.
